{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/comments/1864953221","pull_request_review_id":2471463476,"id":1864953221,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIoc5vKO2F","diff_hunk":"@@ -64,16 +172,131 @@ New features
 
 `scipy.special` improvements
 ============================
-
+- The factorial functions ``special.{factorial,factorial2,factorialk}`` now
+  offer an extension to the complex domain by passing the kwarg
+  ``extend='complex'``. This is opt-in because it changes the values for
+  negative inputs (which by default return 0), as well as for some integers
+  (in the case of ``factorial2`` and ``factorialk``; for more details,
+  check the respective docstrings).
+- `scipy.special.zeta` now defines the Riemann zeta function on the complex
+  plane.
+- `scipy.special.softplus` computes the softplus function
+- The spherical Bessel functions (`scipy.special.spherical_jn`,
+  `scipy.special.spherical_yn`, `scipy.special.spherical_in`, and
+  `scipy.special.spherical_kn`) now support negative arguments with real dtype.
+- `scipy.special.logsumexp` now preserves precision when one element of the
+  sum has magnitude much bigger than the rest.
+- The accuracy of several functions has been improved:
+
+  - `scipy.special.ncfdtr` and `scipy.special.nctdtr` have been improved
+    throughout the domain.
+  - `scipy.special.hyperu` is improved for the case of ``b=1``, small ``x``,
+    and small ``a``.
+  - `scipy.special.logit` is improved near the argument ``p=0.5``.
+  - `scipy.special.rel_entr` is improved when ``x/y`` overflows, underflows,
+    or is close to ``1``.
 
 `scipy.stats` improvements
 ==========================
-
+- A new probability distribution infrastructure has been added for the
+  implementation of univariate, continuous distributions:
+
+  - Speed, accuracy, and memory advantages of the new infastructure are
+    detailed Reference issue
Closes gh-21300

#### What does this implement/fix?
`x` was made keyword-only since it is an optional parameter. However, forcing the user to write (something which will often look like) `simpson(y, x=x)` instead of `simpson(y, x)` seems like an overly-restrictive anti-pattern.

This PR relaxes to allow `x` to be passed positionally. While it isn't 100% obvious that this is the ideal move, there don't seem to be any strong opinions against it in the linked issue - whereas the current state of things has caused confusion for a user.

#### Additional information

Closes gh-21300

#### What does this implement/fix?
`x` was made keyword-only since it is an optional parameter. However, forcing the user to write (something which will often look like) `simpson(y, x=x)` instead of `simpson(y, x)` seems like an overly-restrictive anti-pattern.

This PR relaxes to allow `x` to be passed positionally. While it isn't 100% obvious that this is the ideal move, there don't seem to be any strong opinions against it in the linked issue - whereas the current state of things has caused confusion for a user.

Describe your issue.

Hello,

The call signature of `scipy.integrate.simpson` has a strange `*` as the second argument and specifically requires `simpson(y, x=x)` , as opposed to `simpson(y,x)` as was the case in previous scipy versions and as is presently the case for `scipy.integrate.trapezoid`. This extra `*` argument is [not documented](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.integrate.simpson.html). I suspect this is a bug? In any case, I think having the default second argument as the `x` array would make a lot more sense and would be in line with the call signature of `trapezoid` for example. Below is a minimal working example.

Thank you,
Ankit 

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import numpy as np
from scipy.integrate import simpson

x = np.linspace(0,1,100)
y = x**2

print("Int(y)dx = %.2f" %simpson(y,x))
```


### Error message

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ankit/test.py", line 10, in <module>
    print("Int(y)dx = %.2f" %simpson(y,x))
TypeError: simpson() takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given
```

Customizing leaves ordering in`scipy.cluster.dendrogram` via cut-tree (linkage matrix) reordering

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
Ref. #11060 

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
The objective is to provide the possibility to specify custom leaf ordering in dendrogram. As for now I've chosen a way in which `leaves_order` parameter must be a permutation of indices of original array. 

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
This PR will provide described functionality for any input in which leaves of the dendrogram are the original observations, and in my opinion considering `truncate_mode` option doesn't really make sense.  Time and memory complexity are linear, and that's optimal

#### Reference issue
Closes gh-13125.

#### What does this implement/fix?
Forces LTI state space arrays A, B, C, and D to have inexact type.

Previously, when matrices were all of integer-type, step, lsim, etc. gave unexpected results.

This will restrict StateSpace to float32, float64, complex64, and complex128 types. So, a possible objection to the change is that it would previously have allowed more general types for ABCD, but I don't know if this is intended or required.

Reference issue

Closes #14783.

#### What does this implement/fix?

Function `cyclic_sd` computes the cross-cyclic spectral density, using `csd` 
https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.signal.csd.html

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spectral_correlation_density

- [x] code and doc
- [x] example
- [x] test

#### Additional information

##### Function name

I don't know how to choose the function name.
"cross-cyclic spectral density" should be naturally abbreviated into "csd", 
but this name is already used for "cross-power spectral density".
Ideally, "cross-power spectral density" should have been abbreviated into "cpsd", 
and "cross-cyclic spectral density" into "ccsd"...

##### Example

Example of documentation computes cyclic spectral density of a signal at `f_sig`=28 Hz resulting from amplitude modulation, using a carrier frequency. Then, a noise is added.
![dev_cyclic_fig11](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/5346669/ade15b08-d29c-4850-8c4c-db4191175bb4)


The modulation is expected to occur twice at frequency `f_sig`.
![dev_cyclic_fig12](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/5346669/ba1caa7d-fd83-4012-b7db-9e1ce5544d32)

```diff
--- a/scipy/signal/tests/test_peak_finding.py
+++ b/scipy/signal/tests/test_peak_finding.py
@@ -1,4 +1,5 @@
 import copy

grid","user":{"login":"tylerjereddy","id":7903078,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5MDMwNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7903078?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy","html_url":"https://github.com/tylerjereddy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37342579,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MjU3OQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.interpolate","name":"scipy.interpolate","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""},{"id":38890106,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzODg5MDEwNg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/query","name":"query","color":"d4c5f9","default":false,"description":"A question or suggestion that requires further information"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-11-05T00:37:38Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T20:35:54Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We have an internal use case for `CloughTocher2DInterpolator` with a regular grid where `RegularGridInterpolator` is taking much longer to run, so the user would really like to be able to use `CloughTocher2DInterpolator` with the regular/rectangular grid reproducibly.

The problem, of course, is that a regular grid violates general position, so the Delaunay triangulation used under the hood by `CloughTocher2DInterpolator` is not guaranteed to be unique, and so i.e., our testsuite ceases to be portable on different supercomputers and so on.

I was able to reproduce a pretty substantial difference in the interpolation output for the code below the fold when just running it on i.e., my mac locally vs. a Linux box (with latest SciPy `main`).

<details>

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy
from scipy.interpolate import CloughTocher2DInterpolator

x = np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]) 
y = np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]) 
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y, indexing='ij')
points = np.column_stack([X.ravel(), Y.ravel()])
z = np.sqrt(points[..., 0] + points[..., 1])
X1 = np.linspace(min(x), max(x), num=500)
Y1 = np.linspace(min(y), max(y), num=500)
X1, Y1 = np.meshgrid(X1, Y1)
interp = CloughTocher2DInterpolator(points, z)
Z = interp(X1, Y1)
print("Z:", Z.sum(), Z.min(), Z.max())
```

</details>

- mac: `Z: 644349.4631240882 0.0 3.7416573867739413`
- Linux: `Z: 644324.0908721345 0.0 3.7416573867739413`
(i.e, the first printed value)

If I manually joggle the generators via Qhull, `tri = Delaunay(points, qhull_options="QJ")`, the results appear to be *extremely* close:

- mac: `Z: 644552.4920765815 0.0 3.7416573867739413`
- Linux: `Z: 644552.4920765815 0.0 3.7416573867739413`

The user did indicate that they tried to distort the grid a bit themselves, and it did work sometimes, but depending on the grid density it sometimes was not sufficient for their purposes. I'll point them to this issue to see if this joggling approach suffices, but if not I know they had an interest in being able to provide a custom triangulation of the rectangular grid to `CloughTocher2DInterpolator`. Anyway, I guess let's first see if the joggling suffices for them, and if not I'd be curious to hear suggestions from the `interpolate` folks.

I cast a fairly wide net, adding a few things I thought were borderline, since I figured it would be easier to remove them than to have other also scanning through the pages of PRs.

I also added the array API support section--somehow I forgot that in the first pass at transcribing from the wiki page a few days ago.

Caution: I caught some deprecations that were not mentioned--particularly messy was stuff surrounding `special` like `assoc_legendre_p_all` -- I was about to add a release note saying that `lpn` et al. had a new keyword/improvement, and then when I looked at them, they were deprecated in the same release as the improvement!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2516139697/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21092.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21092.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Hello all,

I encountered an issue in the minimize function while working with differential evolution. For some members of the population, the direc1 value of the extrapolated point became zero without being caught by the check in `_linesearch_powell`, meaning the optimisation could not progress further.

It turns out this problem arises during the iteration of successive points in `_minimize_powell`: In each step, the current point is stored for comparison with the subsequent point, from which the direc1 value for the next iteration is calculated. While the f(x)-value of each point is correctly stored, the corresponding x-value is not stored at all if the condition fx > fx2 is fulfilled. That means the current point will be stored with the correct f(x)-value but with the wrong (previous) x-value.

In some circumstances it can now happen that in one iteration `_linesearch_powell` results in a point with  `2.0 * (fx - fval) > bnd`, meaning the function doesn't break out, but with `x - x1 = direc1 = 0`, meaning the optimisation cannot progress further.

This pull request fixes the storing of x-values, such that all points are stored correctly for comparison with subsequent points of the iteration. Reference issue
Closes gh-19436, gh-20083.

#### What does this implement/fix?
Adds an internal function `special._ufunc._js_div(x,y)` to compute the Jensen Shannon Divergence accurately. In particular, it ensures the result is non-negative. The referenced issues are both caused by negative distance due to numerical (cancellation) error.

`x` and `y` must be real numbers between `0` and `1`, inclusive.

#### Additional information
This PR is intended to supersede PRs gh-19438 and gh-20414. The former one only addresses subnormal input (which does not help gh-20083), and the latter appears to cause [reduced accuracy](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20414#issuecomment-2044225890).

Test cases for the linked issues are added. Both the Python path (via `jensenshannon`) and the C path (via `cdist`) are covered.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20786/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20786/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2519004972","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20786#issuecomment-2519004972","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20786","id":2519004972,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6WJPcs","user":{"login":"tylerjereddy","id":7903078,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5MDMwNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7903078?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy","html_url":"https://github.com/tylerjereddy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-05T03:22:49Z","updated_at":"2024-12-05T03:22:49Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> I’m converting this PR to Draft for the moment as I’m working to consolidate

Reference issue
Toward gh-15928

#### What does this implement/fix?
SciPy distributions like `lognorm`, `loglaplace`, and `loguniform` are examples of distributions underlying random variables whose logarithm is distributed according to a more fundamental distribution. To generalize this idea and avoid naming inconsistencies[^1], this PR adds a class that represents the distribution underlying a strictly monotonic function of a random variable, and a funtion `exp` that takes the exponential of a random variable. For example, if `X = stats.Normal()`, `Y = exp(X)` is lognormally distributed. While I was at it, I went ahead and overrode the `__rtruediv__` operator, so if `X` is gamma-distributed, `Y = 1 / X` is inverse gamma distributed.

#### Additional information
This class makes it easy to define other transformations of random variables including `log` and `ContinuousDistribution.__pow__`[^2]. `log` and `exp` probably shouldn't be public functions of the `stats` subpackage - maybe we need a new namespace? Or make these static methods (or methods?) of a superclass `ProbabilityDistribution`?

[^1]: Confusingly, `loggamma` represents the distribution of a random variable that *is the logarithm* of a gamma-distributed random variable (rather than "whose logarithm is a gamma-distributed random variable").

Reference issue
No issue, just noticed this opportunity while working on another related project.

#### What does this implement/fix?
CSR sparse matrix element-wise binary operators are implemented as templates. The binop operation is fairly large and instantiated many times, so binops account for a significant fraction of both compilation time and library size.

Binops are largely memory bound. That means slightly increasing the cost of the op, even in the inner loop, has little visible performance impact. Therefore the less common operators can be grouped into one implementation with the operand chosen at runtime. This allows two template instantiations to cover all operands. Two because the comparators (greater, less, not_equal_to, etc) return bool while the other operators return T.

Plus and Minus are left alone.

### Build Impact

`_sparsetools.so` goes from 4.0 MB to 2.2 MB.

Total `python build.py dev` runtime drops by about 5%, but the very large`csr.cxx.o` and `bsr.cxx.o` compilation time drops significantly (big red and purple blocks on the bottom rows):

main branch:
![main timeline](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/2730364/88124bf1-6870-4487-bf5e-7ed439edf76f)

this PR:
![smallcsr timeline](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/2730364/3d2e5e2f-d10b-4993-9e0a-66569535f3c6)

These may be the dominating steps for folks with 20+ core machines so those folks may see a noticeable speedup.

### Runtime Impact

I'm seeing up to 5% impact on elementwise multiply on very large (1M+ nnz) matrices, and less on smaller ones. Note that #19765 speeds up these methods by more than this amount.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19766/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":1,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19766/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2521793643","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/19766#issuecomment-2521793643","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19766","id":2521793643,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6WT4Rr","user":{"login":"tylerjereddy","id":7903078,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5MDMwNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7903078?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy","html_url":"https://github.com/tylerjereddy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-06T00:14:27Z","updated_at":"2024-12-06T00:14:27Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"No information"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-11-05T00:37:38Z","updated_at":"2024-12-06T03:14:27Z","closed_at":"2024-12-06T03:14:27Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We have an internal use case for `CloughTocher2DInterpolator` with a regular grid where `RegularGridInterpolator` is taking much longer to run, so the user would really like to be able to use `CloughTocher2DInterpolator` with the regular/rectangular grid reproducibly.

The problem, of course, is that a regular grid violates general position, so the Delaunay triangulation used under the hood by `CloughTocher2DInterpolator` is not guaranteed to be unique, and so i.e., our testsuite ceases to be portable on different supercomputers and so on.

I was able to reproduce a pretty substantial difference in the interpolation output for the code below the fold when just running it on i.e., my mac locally vs. a Linux box (with latest SciPy `main`).

<details>

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy
from scipy.interpolate import CloughTocher2DInterpolator

x = np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]) 
y = np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]) 
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y, indexing='ij')
points = np.column_stack([X.ravel(), Y.ravel()])
z = np.sqrt(points[..., 0] + points[..., 1])
X1 = np.linspace(min(x), max(x), num=500)
Y1 = np.linspace(min(y), max(y), num=500)
X1, Y1 = np.meshgrid(X1, Y1)
interp = CloughTocher2DInterpolator(points, z)
Z = interp(X1, Y1)
print("Z:", Z.sum(), Z.min(), Z.max())
```

</details>

- mac: `Z: 644349.4631240882 0.0 3.7416573867739413`
- Linux: `Z: 644324.0908721345 0.0 3.7416573867739413`
(i.e, the first printed value)

If I manually joggle the generators via Qhull, `tri = Delaunay(points, qhull_options="QJ")`, the results appear to be *extremely* close:

- mac: `Z: 644552.4920765815 0.0 3.7416573867739413`
- Linux: `Z: 644552.4920765815 0.0 3.7416573867739413`

The user did indicate that they tried to distort the grid a bit themselves, and it did work sometimes, but depending on the grid density it sometimes was not sufficient for their purposes. I'll point them to this issue to see if this joggling approach suffices, but if not I know they had an interest in being able to provide a custom triangulation of the rectangular grid to `CloughTocher2DInterpolator`. Anyway, I guess let's first see if the joggling suffices for them, and if not I'd be curious to hear suggestions from the `interpolate` folks.

```diff
--- a/scipy/differentiate/tests/test_differentiate.py
+++ b/scipy/differentiate/tests/test_differentiate.py
@@ -571,7 +571,7 @@ class TestJacobian(JacobianHessianTest):
 
     def test_attrs(self, xp):
         # Test attributes of result object
-        z = xp.asarray([0.5, 0.25])
+        z = xp.asarray([0.5, 0.25], dtype=xp.float64)

the warning category is `None`, and also enforces our intended
warnings behavior for the element guessing changes in MDAnalysisgh-4744.

See discussion related to SPEC 0/community support standards at https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21982#discussion_r1873996863. s://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21092.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21092.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Hello all,

I encountered an issue in the minimize function while working with differential evolution. For some members of the population, the direc1 value of the extrapolated point became zero without being caught by the check in `_linesearch_powell`, meaning the optimisation could not progress further.

It turns out this problem arises during the iteration of successive points in `_minimize_powell`: In each step, the current point is stored for comparison with the subsequent point, from which the direc1 value for the next iteration is calculated. While the f(x)-value of each point is correctly stored, the corresponding x-value is not stored at all if the condition fx > fx2 is fulfilled. That means the current point will be stored with the correct f(x)-value but with the wrong (previous) x-value.

In some circumstances it can now happen that in one iteration `_linesearch_powell` results in a point with  `2.0 * (fx - fval) > bnd`, meaning the function doesn't break out, but with `x - x1 = direc1 = 0`, meaning the optimisation cannot progress further.

This pull request fixes the storing of x-values, such that all points are stored correctly for comparison with subsequent points of the iteration. Additionally, a unit test is implemented to check the correct behaviour.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21092/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21092/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2524748285","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21092#issuecomment-2524748285","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21092","id":2524748285,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6WfJn9","user":{"login":"tylerjereddy","id":7903078,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5MDMwNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7903078?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy","html_url":"https://github.com/tylerjereddy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-07T01:38:32Z","updated_at":"2024-12-07T01:38:32Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Alright, a new optional parameter `callback` to least_squares.
A user-defined function that is called on each iteration, can stop
optimization by returning True or raising StopIteration.
Same signature and API as in eg. scipy.optimize.differential_evolution.
Only implemented for trf and dogleg methods so far.

#### Reference issue
None

#### What does this implement/fix?
Added `callback` feature in scipy.optimize.least_squares

#### Additional information
Forum thread: https://discuss.scientific-python.org/t/add-callbacks-in-scipy-optimize-least-squares/1493

Please describe.

Starting from python 3.13t there is experimental support for free threading where the global interpreter lock (GIL) is disabled.

While `pip install SciPy` works fine with normal python 3.13 it fails with free threading python.

Steps to reproduce:
1) `python3.13t -m venv venv` - here is important to specifically use `python3.13t` instead of `python` as `python` will generate normal environment, but `python3.13t`  setup free threading environment.
2) `.\venv\Scripts\activate.bat`
3) `pip install SciPy`

Reference issue
Follow-up to gh-20989, gh-15618
Closes gh-7099

#### What does this implement/fix?
This extends `fisher_exact` to two-dimensional tables with shapes other than `(2, 2)`. As the notes of `fisher_exact` already explained, there is more than one choice of statistic associated with Fisher's exact test; here, we use the PMF of [`scipy.stats.random_table`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.stats.random_table.html) (with the marginals of the observed table) as the statistic. The p-value - the percentage of tables with the observed marginals that produce a statistic at least as extreme (small) as the observed table - is determined via either a permutation or Monte Carlo approach. Although the two approaches are exactly equivalent in this case, both may be valuable: the Monte Carlo approach is much faster because it uses `scipy.stats.random_table` to sample the tables, but the permutation approach *can* perform a deterministic exact test for sufficiently small tables. Both approaches are exact or almost exact in a long-run sense.

#### Additional information
I'll need to check the behavior for 1 x C and R x 1 tables and maybe add some edge case handling for those. This is the second PR (follow-up to #21197) aiming to extend support for n-D COO arrays in SciPy (refer to https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21169). I am undertaking this work as part of my summer internship with Quansight Labs till the end of September, under the mentorship of @rgommers and @izaid._

This PR aims to add support for n-dimensional matrix multiplication, dot, and tensordot for COO arrays, along with `broadcast_to` which replicates the behaviour of `np.broadcast_to` but for COO arrays.

The approach taken for these methods is as follows -

### `matmul`

#### sparse `a` x sparse `b`
We broadcast `a` and `b`, convert both to block_diagonal form in COO format (using helper function `_block_diag`). We convert the 2-D block diagonal `a` and `b` to 2-D CSR, use `csr_matmat` for 2-D sparse-sparse `matmul`, convert the 2-D block diagonal product to an n-D COO array (using helper function `_extract_block_diag`) to obtain our product.


#### sparse `a` x dense `b`
C++ function `coo_matmat_dense_nd` added to perform sparse-dense `matmul` (this is the n-D extension of 2-D `coo_matmat_dense` which was added at #21240).

 
### `dot`/`tensordot`

#### sparse `a` and sparse `b`
We map n-D COO arrays `a` and `b` to 2-D COO arrays (using `np.ravel_multi_index`), convert to CSR, use `csr_matmat`, then map the 2-D result to an n-D COO array (using `np.unravel_index`), which is the final result.
(thanks to the [proof-of-concept](https://gist.github.com/dschult/6b65bd18cd425d07b1a659eb7f67bc58) given by @dschult, mentioned at https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21169#issuecomment-2231275707)

The case of `tensordot` is similar, though it involved some additional transposing based on the `axes` input.


#### sparse `a` and dense `b`
The implementation for sparse-dense `dot` involved reshaping `a` and `b` and using `matmul`. The reshaping can be described as follows -

For `a` of shape `(2,3,4,5)` and `b` shape `(6,7,5,9)` (where `a.shape[-1] == b.shape[-2]` for `dot`), `a.dot(b)` will have shape `(2,3,4,6,7,9)`.
If we reshape `a` to `(2,3,4,1,1,1)` and `b` to `(1,1,1,6,7,9)` and multiply the reshaped arrays after broadcasting, we get a product of shape `(2,3,4,6,7,9)`, which is equal to the dot of the original `a` and `b`.

The case for `tensordot` is once again similar, but required some transposing first.



I have also added extensive testing for the added and modified methods.
(**Note** - diff may be easier to view once #21197 has been merged, as currently it includes all the changes introduced in the first PR Reference issue
Toward gh-15928

#### What does this implement/fix?
SciPy distributions like `lognorm`, `loglaplace`, and `loguniform` are examples of distributions underlying random variables whose logarithm is distributed according to a more fundamental distribution. To generalize this idea and avoid naming inconsistencies[^1], this PR adds a class that represents the distribution underlying a strictly monotonic function of a random variable, and a funtion `exp` that takes the exponential of a random variable. For example, if `X = stats.Normal()`, `Y = exp(X)` is lognormally distributed. While I was at it, I went ahead and overrode the `__rtruediv__` operator, so if `X` is gamma-distributed, `Y = 1 / X` is inverse gamma distributed.

#### Additional information
This class makes it easy to define other transformations of random variables including `log` and `ContinuousDistribution.__pow__`[^2]. `log` and `exp` probably shouldn't be public functions of the `stats` subpackage - maybe we need a new namespace? Or make these static methods (or methods?) of a superclass `ProbabilityDistribution`?

[^1]: Confusingly, `loggamma` represents the distribution of a random variable that *is the logarithm* of a gamma-distributed random variable (rather than "whose logarithm is a gamma-distributed random variable").
[^2]: `__pow__` will require a little more work because taking even powers of a distribution whose support includes negative numbers is not an injective Reference issue
gh-9439

#### What does this implement/fix?
Adds the ability to truncate the distribution underlying a random variable. Truncation can be applied before or after other transformations, which makes it easier to get the desired result.

```python3
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import Normal, truncate

X = Normal()

# Truncate shifted/scaled variable (new)
Y = truncate(2*X + 1, lb=-2, ub=2)
Y.plot()
plt.show()
```
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8489d082-4678-4313-9fd5-9c2519d192a6)

```python3
# Shift/scale truncated variable (like `truncnorm`)
Y = 2*truncate(X, lb=-2, ub=2) + 1
Y.plot()
plt.show()
```
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e9c5f0e2-4155-4255-ad9e-c390394a4fd0)

#### Additional information
This will not be quite as robust as `truncnorm` as written, but I can look at improving it / testing it with `truncnorm` tests as a Reference issue
Toward gh-15928

#### What does this implement/fix?
This adds a class representing the distribution of order statistics of samples drawn from another distribution. 

```python3
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

rng = np.random.default_rng(7546349802439582)
n = 5
r = np.arange(1, n+1)
X = stats.Normal()
Y = stats.OrderStatisticDistribution(X, n=n, r=r)
x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 300)[:, np.newaxis]
plt.plot(x, Y.pdf(x))
plt.legend([f'$r={str(ri)}$' for ri in r])
plt.title("Distribution of Normal Order Statistics, $n=5$")
plt.show()

Y.mean()
array([-1.16296447e+00, -4.95018970e-01,  1.27338829e-21,  4.95018970e-01, 1.16296447e+00])
```
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a1771bb3-c15f-438e-89cb-5e87539ee1a0)

#### Additional information
Along with gh-19475, this paves the way toward fitting distributions to data using the method of l-moments. In principle, it could also be used to define a Shapiro-Wilk-like statistics (for goodness-of-fit tests involving distributions other than the normal).

two similar helper functions from `sparse.linalg` and `csgraph._tools.py` and put it in `sparse._sputils.py`. These helper functions provide safe downcasting of sparse index arrays which will presumably be needed by other libraries. 

The new public function is `safely_cast_index_arrays(A, idx_dtype)`. 
- It raises a ValueError if casting is not safe. 
- It returns recast index arrays rather than making the change "in-place".  
- If no casting is needed, the original arrays are returned. Checking `original is not result` tells if casting was done.
- An optional `msg` input is added to the error messages to inform why the restriction is needed (e.g. SuperLU, or csgraph, etc).

Question:
- would this be better as a sparse method? The code essentially splits into handling each format. But a single function was easier to write and then split to methods if desired.
- should I remove the optional `msg` input and use a generic ValueError message? I only included it because the linalg error messages said casting was needed for SuperLU, and that doesn't work for csgraph. This allows users to customize the error messages. Reference issue
Toward gh-15928
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/79166209/how-to-generate-normally-distributed-sobol-sequences-arrays-in-python/79168966#79168966

#### What does this implement/fix?
This enables generation of quasi-random samples from statistical distributions.

<details>

```python3
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats
X = stats.Normal()
qrng = stats.qmc.Sobol(1)
bins = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 31)
plt.hist(X.sample(512, rng=qrng), bins, alpha=0.5, label='quasi-random')
plt.hist(X.sample(512, rng=qrng.rng), bins, alpha=0.5, label='pseudo-random')
plt.title('Histogram of normally-distributed sample')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

</details>

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/382b1ca2-6d02-46c8-a34c-d9c7af63232c)

#### Additional information
How the `shape` parameter is handled:

> If `rng` is an instance of `scipy.stats.QMCEngine` configured to use scrambling and `shape` is not empty, then each slice along the zeroth axis of the result is a "quasi-independent", low-discrepancy sequence; that is, they are distinct sequences that can be treated as statistically independent for most practical purposes. Separate calls to `sample` produce new quasi-independent, low-discrepancy sequences.

So if `len(shape) > 1`, we do this by spawning new RNGs (e.g. `math.prod(shape[1:])` of them if the random variable's parameters are all scalars), instantiatiating a `QMCEngine` with each, and drawing a sample of length `shape[0]` from each of the `QMCEngine`s before passing them through the inverse CDF. Slow, but that's the best we can do with the existing `QMCEngine` interface, I Don't merge until the `1.15.0` release notes updates have been merged and the `maintenance/1.15.x` branch has been pushed up (I'll probably merge when the time is right).

* The changed dependency versions in the `1.16.0` release notes are just drafts/subject to change. Note that I've not taken on the task of actually updating those dependencies in places where they may get checked programmatically (i.e., `pyproject.toml`), since that may be a larger project that can happen after branching.

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

 [build-system]
 build-backend = 'mesonpy'
 requires = [
-    "meson-python>=0.15.0",
-    "Cython>=3.0.8",        # when updating version, also update check in meson.build
-    "pybind11>=2.13.2",     # when updating version, also update check in scipy/meson.build
-    "pythran>=0.14.0",
+    # The upper bound on meson-python is pre-emptive only (looser
+    # on purpose, since chance of breakage in 0.18/0.19 is low with
+    # 0.17.1 working at time of writing)
+    "meson-python>=0.15.0,<0.20.0",
+    # we need at least Cython 3.1.0a1 for free-threaded CPython
+    # 3.13 wheel builds
+    "Cython>=3.1.0a1,<3.2.0; python_version=='3.13t'",        # when updating version, also update check in meson.build
+    # for other CPython versions, the regular pre-emptive
+    # Cython version bounds policy applies

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

 [build-system]
 build-backend = 'mesonpy'
 requires = [
-    "meson-python>=0.15.0",
-    "Cython>=3.0.8",        # when updating version, also update check in meson.build
-    "pybind11>=2.13.2",     # when updating version, also update check in scipy/meson.build
-    "pythran>=0.14.0",
+    # The upper bound on meson-python is pre-emptive only (looser
+    # on purpose, since chance of breakage in 0.18/0.19 is low with
+    # 0.17.1 working at time of writing)
+    "meson-python>=0.15.0,<0.20.0",
+    # we need at least Cython 3.1.0a1 for free-threaded CPython
+    # 3.13 wheel builds
+    "Cython>=3.1.0a1,<3.2.0; python_version=='3.13t'",        # when updating version, also update check in meson.build
+    # for other CPython versions, the regular pre-emptive
+    # Cython version bounds policy applies
+    "Cython>=3.0.8,<3.1.0; python_version!='3.13t'",
+    # The upper bound on pybind11 is pre-emptive only
+    "pybind11>=2.13.2,<2.14.0",     # when updating version, also update check in scipy/meson.build
+    # The upper bound on pythran is pre-emptive only; 0.17.0
+    # is released/working at time of writing, and 0.18.x may
+    # have desirable free-threaded CPython 3.13 support
+ CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

+    # The upper bound on meson-python is pre-emptive only (looser

+    "meson-python>=0.15.0,<0.20.0",
+    # we need at least Cython 3.1.0a1 for free-threaded CPython

+    "Cython>=3.1.0a1,<3.2.0; python_version=='3.13t'",        # when updating version, also update check in meson.build
+    # for other CPython versions, the regular pre-emptive
+    # Cython version bounds policy applies
+    "Cython>=3.0.8,<3.1.0; python_version!='3.13t'",
+    # The upper bound on pybind11 is pre-emptive only

+    # The upper bound on pythran is pre-emptive only; 0.17.0
+    # is released/working at time of writing, and 0.18.x may
+    # have desirable free-threaded CPython 3.13 support
+    "pythran>=0.14.0,<0.19.0",
 
     # numpy requirement for wheel builds for distribution on PyPI - building

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

- scikit-learn: https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/main/pyproject.toml#L151

> I have no idea what ruff is.

That's fair, their tooling (https://astral.sh/) is nice. CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` free-threaded wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

2.2 changed the rules for abbreviation of large arrays:

```
In [4]: np.arange(5000)
Out[4]: array([   0,    1,    2, ..., 4997, 4998, 4999], shape=(5000,))
```

the `shape=` part is new. This change needs two workarounds:
-  `scipy-doctest` needs to learn how to interpret this fake `shape=` kwarg --- https://github.com/scipy/scipy_doctest/pull/176
- our docstrings need to add the shape info (this PR).

https://github.com/scipy/scipy_doctest/pull/176 tries handle both "new" and "old" representations. This way, once 
scipy-doctest made a release, we and other doctest users only need to add `shapes=` once, and all doctests will work with both numpy < 2.2 and >= CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` free-threaded wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

Reference issue
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21496#discussion_r1878681049

#### What does this implement/fix?
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21496#discussion_r1878681049 describes a bug in which the abscissae/weight pairs of `tanhsinh` get "compressed" along with other elements of the `work` object. 

The first commit attempts to provide a MWE as a unit test (so tests should fail). The second commit will fix the bug by nesting the abscissae/weight pair in another object so they do not get compressed with other arrays. It will also improve the `_elementwise_iterative_method` documentation to highlight this behavior of the `work` object.

#### Additional information
As the code already notes, we could improve the way the code detects whether arrays should be compressed, but that can wait until after release; this can't.

tests all have skip markers for editable installs, which are in some ways "conceptually" related to disabling build isolation. Indeed, both suffer the same fate via `_test_cython_extension` failing to find the `pxd` files on the Cython command that gets called.

I'm messing around a bit with the `meson` native file used in that testing utility to see if I can force a pass-through of the appropriate include paths for Cython, but the Cython lang args seem to be ignored/unused on the Cython incantations. Regardless of whether that syntax is correct for Cython itself, `meson` isn't pass it through to Cython.

For example,

```diff
     with open(native_file, 'w') as f:
         f.write("[binaries]\n")
-        f.write(f"python = '{sys.executable}'")
+        f.write(f"python = '{sys.executable}'\n")
+        f.write("[built-in options]\n")
+        f.write(f"cython_args = ['-I {root_dir}']\n")
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2537528437/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

tests","user":{"login":"tylerjereddy","id":7903078,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5MDMwNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7903078?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy","html_url":"https://github.com/tylerjereddy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"* Temporarily skip the tests flagged in gh-22039 on free-threaded Windows CPython. Note that free-threading itself may be a red herring here, and the issue may ultimately be some combination of the disabling of build isolation for free-threaded wheels compounded with the Windows platform.

* Note that the error messages reported by these test meson builds are identical to those reported by in-place builds of SciPy, for which we already have skips on these tests. Even for the case of the in-place build test failure, which simplifies down to a single-line reproducer, `cimport scipy.linalg`, we still don't have a workaround, so it seems like we may not want to hold up the release candidate for an even more challenging-to-reproduce scenario that has the same error message. Also, if there were fundamental issues with free threading on this platform/Python combination, they would presumably show up somewhere other than just these in-place-sensitive tests.

tests","user":{"login":"tylerjereddy","id":7903078,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5MDMwNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7903078?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy","html_url":"https://github.com/tylerjereddy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"* Temporarily skip the tests flagged in gh-22039 on free-threaded Windows CPython. Note that free-threading itself may be a red herring here, and the issue may ultimately be some combination of the disabling of build isolation for free-threaded wheels compounded with the Windows platform.

* Note that the error messages reported by these test meson builds are identical to those reported by in-place builds of SciPy, for which we already have skips on these tests. Even for the case of the in-place build test failure, which simplifies down to a single-line reproducer, `cimport scipy.linalg`, we still don't have a workaround, so it seems like we may not want to hold up the release candidate for an even more challenging-to-reproduce scenario that has the same error message. Also, if there were fundamental issues with free threading on this platform/Python combination, they would presumably show up somewhere other than just these in-place-sensitive tests.

CI is skipped completely for now because this will not work--it mostly demonstrates what I'd *like* to be able to do, but that apparently cannot be done because of the limited hooks available in https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers. I'm pretty sure our only option at the moment for free-threaded CPython `3.13` wheels will be to treat `pyproject.toml` more or less like free-threaded CPython doesn't exist, and disable build isolation for `cibuildwheel` (which is basically what we do on `main`). 

However, if you wanted to do a more formally-correct build-isolation-enabled CPython `3.13` free-threaded wheel build it looks like you'd be in trouble at the moment?

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release, paying special attention to the complications related to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading support, there are also some version handling changes that mean this update differs from the previous one performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.

{"repository_id":1460385,"push_id":21683181246,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/maintenance/1.15.x","head":"31d7497817ee9f1b9e4e5efca4dd98d1cfd63075","before":"a3e07fbaaaedde1469e5d15bffecac5785258306","commits":[{"sha":"31d7497817ee9f1b9e4e5efca4dd98d1cfd63075","author":{"email":"05cdbc50859d0142332b1c0e4edb7acdfab51a3a@gmail.com","name":"Tyler Reddy"},"message":"MAINT: version pins/prep for 1.15.0rc1 (#22024)

* Update dependency version ranges for build/runtime
as we prepare for the SciPy `1.15.0rc1` pre-release,
paying special attention to the complications related
to free-threaded CPython `3.13` support.

* In addition to the differences caused by free-threading
support, there are also some version handling changes
that mean this update differs from the previous one
performed in the last release cycle at gh-20831.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/commits/31d7497817ee9f1b9e4e5efca4dd98d1cfd63075"}]}

Introduction
Rebase, reorganization, and code review of [PR 17019](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17019).

#### Original description
Another pull request to speed up sparse.csgraph.dijkstra, succeeding https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/16696
Thank you @jjerphan for reviewing the previous pull request and giving me advice

* Replaces FibonacciHeap with std::priority_queue which makes sparse.csgraph.dijkstra faster
   * asymptotically due to the removal of a bug in FibonacciHeap implementation
   * with regard to the constant factor due to better memory usage(?)
* Unifies the four similar dijkstra functions into one
   * Performance overhead was negligible, probably because the priority_queue is the bottleneck
* Adds the star graph to the benchmark as the worst case for the previous version
* Fixes a typo in the test file name
* A part of the test file for dijkstra is modified for better readability, nothing really changed
( See benchmarks in [PR 17019](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17019)).

### Changes from original PR
* Squashed tiny commits into larger commits (e.g., [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17019/commits/90b5151b766f8d68c74fa0f8b5ca6ecd62eb7bf5)).
* Removing irrelevant commits due to changes in repo since the original PR was opened ( e.g., [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17019/commits/92b53181f1fcdb90ec8d3ef2285195e2271c2d55) one).
* Adapted Yen's algorithm to the new Dijkstra internal implementation (under [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/commit/24fddcd7f7d0e8bf65724583515f86361266e7ff) commit).
* Removed random testing ( as I agree with the comment [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17800#pullrequestreview-1251442515)), instead added a star-graph test.

We should probably draw the line on those at RC1 though--I think I'd almost certainly deny an `ENH` backport at RC2.

- [ ] there are two doc build warnings/issue with `scipy.stats` locally, so I'm expecting those to show up here, and indeed `stats` is the subpackage that is seeing the most "debatable" backport activity here because of the flurry of activity around the branch point with the new dist infra
- [ ] Some backport PRs have not been merged yet, including some potentially important `stats` documentation?
  - gh-22025
  - gh-22043


Misc. changes (so far):
1. Increase cirrus CI timeout for wheel builds, was a problem in the last backport PR I did on the release branch


Backports included (so far -- see bold text where I had to resolve merge conflicts manually):

`get_index_dtype` and `safely_cast_index_arrays` public functions in the `scipy.sparse` namespace.

These functions are useful for (re-)setting the dtype of the index arrays in sparse formats BSR, COO, CSC, CSR, and DIA. They are more important than previously because of the migration from `spmatrix` to `sparray`, and the related change to remove automatic value-based dtype selection for index arrays in the array constructors.  

The functions are used in example code in the Migration Guide from spmatrix to sparray.  Other sputils functions are referred to in the guide as part of suggestions for testing during the migration. But only for local and temporary testing to identify features for migration -- not as suggestions for new code to enact the migration. Those sputils are not intended to become public.

This PR includes suggested wording changes to the release notes for v1.15 and I've added a label for `backport-candidate`. The release note wording is a separate commit if someone needs to remove/change it. {"action":"closed","number":22043,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/22043","id":2227097164,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABZIoc6Evs5M","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22043","diff_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22043.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22043.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/22043","number":22043,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"ENH: sparse: make two sputils public for easier index array casting","user":{"login":"dschult","id":915037,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjkxNTAzNw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/915037?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/dschult","html_url":"https://github.com/dschult","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dschult/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dschult/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dschult/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dschult/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dschult/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dschult/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dschult/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dschult/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dschult/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Make `get_index_dtype` and `safely_cast_index_arrays` public functions in the `scipy.sparse` namespace.

These functions are useful for (re-)setting the dtype of the index arrays in sparse formats BSR, COO, CSC, CSR, and DIA. They are more important than previously because of the migration from `spmatrix` to `sparray`, and the related change to remove automatic value-based dtype selection for index arrays in the array constructors.  

The functions are used in example code in the Migration Guide from spmatrix to sparray.  Other sputils functions are referred to in the guide as part of suggestions for testing during the migration. But only for local and temporary testing to identify features for migration -- not as suggestions for new code to enact the migration. Those sputils are not intended to become public.

This PR includes suggested wording changes to the release notes for v1.15 and I've added a label for `backport-candidate`. The release note wording is a separate commit if someone needs to remove/change it. `get_index_dtype` and `safely_cast_index_arrays` public functions in the `scipy.sparse` namespace.

These functions are useful for (re-)setting the dtype of the index arrays in sparse formats BSR, COO, CSC, CSR, and DIA. They are more important than previously because of the migration from `spmatrix` to `sparray`, and the related change to remove automatic value-based dtype selection for index arrays in the array constructors.  

The functions are used in example code in the Migration Guide from spmatrix to sparray.  Other sputils functions are referred to in the guide as part of suggestions for testing during the migration. But only for local and temporary testing to identify features for migration -- not as suggestions for new code to enact the migration. Those sputils are not intended to become public.

This PR includes suggested wording changes to the release notes for v1.15 and I've added a label for `backport-candidate`. The release note wording is a separate commit if someone needs to remove/change it. We should probably draw the line on those at RC1 though--I think I'd almost certainly deny an `ENH` backport at RC2.

* Increase the cirrus CI timeout limit
because wheel builds are running out of
time in the `maintenance/1.15.x` release
branch.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/commits/218688967e593cab59fb53334aaa3a51d23c0056"},{"sha":"582dec3761376ba2413cc6a59f1c3dd531d3b97d","author":{"email":"05cdbc50859d0142332b1c0e4edb7acdfab51a3a@gmail.com","name":"Tyler Reddy"},"message":"TST: temp skip of extending tests

* Temporarily skip the tests flagged in gh-22039
on free-threaded Windows CPython. Note that free-threading
itself may be a red herring here, and the issue may ultimately
be some combination of the disabling of build isolation
for free-threaded wheels compounded with the Windows platform.

* Note that the error messages reported by these test meson
builds are identical to those reported by in-place builds
of SciPy, for which we already have skips on these tests.
Even for the case of the in-place build test failure,
which simplifies down to a single-line reproducer, `cimport scipy.linalg`,
we still don't have a work around, so it seems like we may
not want to hold up the release candidate for an even more
challenging-to-reproduce scenario that has the same error message.
Also, if there were fundamental issues with free threading
on this platform/Python combination, they would presumably
show up somewhere other than just these in-place-sensitive
tests.
