following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"content_type":"application/x-gzip","state":"uploaded","size":59145455,"download_count":0,"created_at":"2024-12-14T00:30:06Z","updated_at":"2024-12-14T00:30:37Z","browser_download_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/releases/download/v1.15.0rc1/scipy-1.15.0rc1.tar.gz"}],"tarball_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/tarball/v1.15.0rc1","zipball_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/zipball/v1.15.0rc1","body":"SciPy 1.15.0 Release Notes\r\n=====================\r\n\r\n**Note**: SciPy `1.15.0` is not released yet!\r\n\r\nSciPy `1.15.0` is the culmination of `6` months of hard work. It contains\r\nmany new features, numerous bug-fixes, improved test coverage and better\r\ndocumentation. There have been a number of deprecations and API changes\r\nin this release, which are documented below. All users are encouraged to\r\nupgrade to this release, as there are a large number of bug-fixes and\r\noptimizations. Before upgrading, we recommend that users check that\r\ntheir own code does not use deprecated SciPy functionality (to do so,\r\nrun your code with ``python -Wd`` and check for ``DeprecationWarning`` s).\r\nOur development attention will now shift to bug-fix releases on the\r\n1.15.x branch, and on adding new features on the main branch.\r\n\r\nThis release requires Python `3.10-3.13` and NumPy `1.23.5` or greater.\r\n\r\n\r\nHighlights of this release\r\n===================\r\n\r\n- Sparse arrays are now fully functional for 1-D and 2-D arrays. We recommend\r\n  that all new code use sparse arrays instead of sparse matrices and that\r\n  developers start to migrate their existing code from sparse matrix to sparse\r\n  array: [ `migration_to_sparray`](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/sparse.migration_to_sparray.html). Both ``sparse.linalg`` and ``sparse.csgraph``\r\n  work with either sparse matrix or sparse array and work internally with\r\n  sparse array.\r\n- Sparse arrays now provide basic support for n-D arrays in the COO format\r\n  including ``add``, ``subtract``, ``reshape``, ``transpose``, ``matmul``,\r\n  ``dot``, ``tensordot`` and others. More functionality is coming in future\r\n  releases.\r\n- Preliminary support for free-threaded Python 3.13.\r\n- New probability distribution features in `scipy.stats` can be used to improve\r\n  the speed and accuracy of existing continuous distributions and perform new\r\n  probability calculations.\r\n- `scipy.differentiate` is a new top-level submodule for accurate\r\n  estimation of derivatives of black box functions.\r\n- `scipy.optimize.elementwise` provides vectorized root-finding and\r\n  minimization of univariate functions, and it supports the array API\r\n  as do new ``integrate`` functions ``tanhsinh``, ``nsum``, and ``cubature``.\r\n- `scipy.interpolate.AAA` adds the AAA algorithm for barycentric rational\r\n  approximation of real or complex functions.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nNew features\r\n==========\r\n\r\n`scipy.differentiate` introduction\r\n=========================\r\nThe new `scipy.differentiate` sub-package contains functions for accurate\r\nestimation of derivatives of black box functions.\r\n\r\n* Use `scipy.differentiate.derivative` for first-order derivatives of\r\n  scalar-in, scalar-out functions.\r\n* Use `scipy.differentiate.jacobian` for first-order partial derivatives of\r\n  vector-in, vector-out functions.\r\n* Use `scipy.differentiate.hessian` for second-order partial derivatives of\r\n  vector-in, scalar-out functions.\r\n\r\nAll functions use high-order finite difference rules with adaptive (real)\r\nstep size. To facilitate batch computation, these functions are vectorized\r\nand support several Array API compatible array libraries in addition to NumPy\r\n(see \"Array API Standard Support\" below).\r\n\r\n`scipy.integrate` improvements\r\n========================\r\n- The ``QUADPACK`` Fortran77 package has been ported to C.\r\n- `scipy.integrate.lebedev_rule` computes abscissae and weights for\r\n  integration over the surface of a sphere.\r\n- `scipy.integrate.nsum` evaluates finite and infinite series and their\r\n  logarithms.\r\n- `scipy.integrate.tanhsinh` is now exposed for public use, allowing\r\n  evaluation of a convergent integral using tanh-sinh quadrature.\r\n- The new `scipy.integrate.cubature` function supports multidimensional\r\n  integration, and has support for approximating integrals with\r\n  one or more sets of infinite limits.\r\n\r\n\r\n`scipy.interpolate` improvements\r\n===========================\r\n- `scipy.interpolate.AAA` adds the AAA algorithm for barycentric rational\r\n  approximation of real or complex functions.\r\n- `scipy.interpolate.FloaterHormannInterpolator` adds barycentric rational\r\n  interpolation.\r\n- New functions `scipy.interpolate.make_splrep` and\r\n  `scipy.interpolate.make_splprep` implement construction of smoothing splines.\r\n  The algorithmic content is equivalent to FITPACK (``splrep`` and ``splprep``\r\n  functions, and ``*UnivariateSpline`` classes) and the user API is consistent\r\n  with ``make_interp_spline``: these functions receive data arrays and return\r\n  a `scipy.interpolate.BSpline` instance.\r\n- New generator function `scipy.interpolate.generate_knots` implements the\r\n  FITPACK strategy for selecting knots of a smoothing spline given the\r\n  smoothness parameter, ``s``. The function exposes the internal logic of knot\r\n  selection that ``splrep`` and ``*UnivariateSpline`` was using.\r\n\r\n\r\n`scipy.linalg` improvements\r\n======================\r\n- `scipy.linalg.interpolative` Fortran77 code has been ported to Cython.\r\n- `scipy.linalg.solve` supports several new values for the ``assume_a``\r\n  argument, enabling faster computation for diagonal, tri-diagonal, banded, and\r\n  triangular matrices. Also, when ``assume_a`` is left unspecified, the\r\n  function now automatically detects and exploits diagonal, tri-diagonal,\r\n  and triangular structures.\r\n- `scipy.linalg` matrix creation functions (`scipy.linalg.circulant`,\r\n  `scipy.linalg.companion`, `scipy.linalg.convolution_matrix`,\r\n  `scipy.linalg.fiedler`, `scipy.linalg.fiedler_companion`, and\r\n  `scipy.linalg.leslie`) now support batch\r\n  matrix creation.\r\n- `scipy.linalg.funm` is faster.\r\n- `scipy.linalg.orthogonal_procrustes` now supports complex input.\r\n- Wrappers for the following LAPACK routines have been added in\r\n  `scipy.linalg.lapack`: ``?lantr``, ``?sytrs``, ``?hetrs``, ``?trcon``,\r\n  and ``?gtcon``.\r\n- `scipy.linalg.expm` was rewritten in C.\r\n- `scipy.linalg.null_space` now accepts the new arguments ``overwrite_a``,\r\n  ``check_finite``, and ``lapack_driver``.\r\n- ``id_dist`` Fortran code was rewritten in Cython.\r\n\r\n\r\n`scipy.ndimage` improvements\r\n========================\r\n- Several additional filtering functions now support an ``axes`` argument\r\n  that specifies which axes of the input filtering is to be performed on.\r\n  These include ``correlate``, ``convolve``, ``generic_laplace``, ``laplace``,\r\n  ``gaussian_laplace``, ``derivative2``, ``generic_gradient_magnitude``,\r\n  ``gaussian_gradient_magnitude`` and ``generic_filter``.\r\n- The binary and grayscale morphology functions now support an ``axes``\r\n  argument that specifies which axes of the input filtering is to be performed\r\n  on.\r\n- `scipy.ndimage.rank_filter` time complexity has improved from ``n`` to\r\n  ``log(n)``.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\n`scipy.optimize` improvements\r\n========================\r\n- The vendored HiGHS library has been upgraded from ``1.4.0`` to ``1.8.0``,\r\n  bringing accuracy and performance improvements to solvers.\r\n- The ``MINPACK`` Fortran77 package has been ported to C.\r\n- The ``L-BFGS-B`` Fortran77 package has been ported to C.\r\n- The new `scipy.optimize.elementwise` namespace includes functions\r\n  ``bracket_root``, ``find_root``, ``bracket_minimum``, and ``find_minimum``\r\n  for root-finding and minimization of univariate functions. To facilitate\r\n  batch computation, these functions are vectorized and support several\r\n  Array API compatible array libraries in addition to NumPy (see\r\n  \"Array API Standard Support\" below). Compared to existing functions (e.g.\r\n  `scipy.optimize.root_scalar` and `scipy.optimize.minimize_scalar`),\r\n  these functions can offer speedups of over 100x when used with NumPy arrays,\r\n  and even greater gains are possible with other Array API Standard compatible\r\n  array libraries (e.g. CuPy).\r\n- `scipy.optimize.differential_evolution` now supports more general use of\r\n  ``workers``, such as passing a map-like callable.\r\n- `scipy.optimize.nnls` was rewritten in Cython.\r\n- ``HessianUpdateStrategy`` now supports ``__matmul__``.\r\n\r\n\r\n`scipy.signal` improvements\r\n======================\r\n- Add functionality of complex-valued waveforms to ``signal.chirp()``.\r\n- `scipy.signal.lombscargle` has two new arguments, ``weights`` and\r\n  ``floating_mean``, enabling sample weighting and removal of an unknown\r\n  y-offset independently for each frequency. Additionally, the ``normalize``\r\n  argument includes a new option to return the complex representation of the\r\n  amplitude and phase.\r\n- New function `scipy.signal.envelope` for computation of the envelope of a\r\n  real or complex valued signal.\r\n\r\n\r\n`scipy.sparse` improvements\r\n=======================\r\n- A :ref:`migration guide<migration_to_sparray>` is now available for\r\n  moving from sparse.matrix to sparse.array in your code/library.\r\n- Sparse arrays now support indexing for 1-D and 2-D arrays. So, sparse\r\n  arrays are now fully functional for 1-D and 2D.\r\n- n-D sparse arrays in COO format can now be constructed, reshaped and used\r\n  for basic arithmetic.\r\n- New functions ``sparse.linalg.is_sptriangular`` and\r\n  ``sparse.linalg.spbandwidth`` mimic the existing dense tools\r\n  ``linalg.is_triangular`` and ``linalg.bandwidth``.\r\n- ``sparse.linalg`` and ``sparse.csgraph`` now work with sparse arrays. Be\r\n  careful that your index arrays are 32-bit. We are working on 64bit support.\r\n- The vendored ``ARPACK`` library has been upgraded to version ``3.9.1``.\r\n- COO, CSR, CSC and LIL formats now support the ``axis`` argument for\r\n  ``count_nonzero``.\r\n- Sparse arrays and matrices may now raise errors when initialized with\r\n  incompatible data types, such as ``float16``.\r\n- ``min``, ``max``, ``argmin``, and ``argmax`` now support computation\r\n  over nonzero elements only via the new ``explicit`` argument.\r\n- New functions ``get_index_dtype`` and ``safely_cast_index_arrays`` are\r\n  available to facilitate index array casting in ``sparse``.\r\n\r\n\r\n`scipy.spatial` improvements\r\n======================\r\n- ``Rotation.concatenate`` now accepts a bare ``Rotation`` object, and will\r\n  return a copy of it.\r\n\r\n\r\n`scipy.special` improvements\r\n========================\r\n- The factorial functions ``special.{factorial,factorial2,factorialk}`` now\r\n  offer an extension to the complex domain by passing the kwarg\r\n  ``extend='complex'``. This is opt-in because it changes the values for\r\n  negative inputs (which by default return 0), as well as for some integers\r\n  (in the case of ``factorial2`` and ``factorialk``; for more details,\r\n  check the respective docstrings).\r\n- `scipy.special.zeta` now defines the Riemann zeta function on the complex\r\n  plane.\r\n- `scipy.special.softplus` computes the softplus function\r\n- The spherical Bessel functions (`scipy.special.spherical_jn`,\r\n  `scipy.special.spherical_yn`, `scipy.special.spherical_in`, and\r\n  `scipy.special.spherical_kn`) now support negative arguments with real dtype.\r\n- `scipy.special.logsumexp` now preserves precision when one element of the\r\n  sum has magnitude much bigger than the rest.\r\n- The accuracy of several functions has been improved:\r\n\r\n  - `scipy.special.ncfdtr` and `scipy.special.nctdtr` have been improved\r\n    throughout the domain.\r\n  - `scipy.special.hyperu` is improved for the case of ``b=1``, small ``x``,\r\n    and small ``a``.\r\n  - `scipy.special.logit` is improved near the argument ``p=0.5``.\r\n  - `scipy.special.rel_entr` is improved when ``x/y`` overflows, underflows,\r\n    or is close to ``1``.\r\n\r\n- `scipy.special.gdtrib` may now be used in a CuPy ``ElementwiseKernel`` on\r\n  GPUs.\r\n- `scipy.special.ndtr` is now more efficient.\r\n\r\n`scipy.stats` improvements\r\n=====================\r\n- A new probability distribution infrastructure has been added for the\r\n  implementation of univariate, continuous distributions with speed,\r\n  accuracy, and memory advantages:\r\n\r\n  - `scipy.stats.Normal` represents the normal distribution with the new\r\n    interface. In typical cases, its methods are faster and more accurate than\r\n    those of `scipy.stats.norm`.\r\n  - Use `scipy.stats.make_distribution` to treat an existing continuous\r\n    distribution (e.g. `scipy.stats.norm`) with the new infrastructure.\r\n    This can improve the speed and accuracy of existing distributions,\r\n    especially for methods not overridden with custom formulas in the\r\n    implementation.\r\n\r\n- `scipy.stats.Mixture` has been added to represent mixture distributions.\r\n- Instances of `scipy.stats.Normal` and the classes returned by\r\n  `scipy.stats.make_distribution` are supported by several new mathematical\r\n  transformations.\r\n\r\n  - `scipy.stats.truncate` for truncation of the support.\r\n  - `scipy.stats.order_statistic` for the order statistics of a given number\r\n    of IID random variables.\r\n  - `scipy.stats.abs`, `scipy.stats.exp`, and `scipy.stats.log`. For example,\r\n    ``scipy.stats.abs(Normal())`` is distributed according to the folded normal\r\n    and ``scipy.stats.exp(Normal())`` is lognormally distributed.\r\n\r\n- The new `scipy.stats.lmoment` calculates sample l-moments and l-moment\r\n  ratios. Notably, these sample estimators are unbiased.\r\n- `scipy.stats.chatterjeexi` computes the Xi correlation coefficient, which\r\n  can detect nonlinear dependence. The function also performs a hypothesis\r\n  test of independence between samples.\r\n- `scipy.stats.wilcoxon` has improved method resolution logic for the default\r\n  ``method='auto'``. Other values of ``method`` provided by the user are now\r\n  respected in all cases, and the method argument ``approx`` has been\r\n  renamed to ``asymptotic`` for consistency with similar functions. (Use of\r\n  ``approx`` is still allowed for backward compatibility.)\r\n- There are several new probability distributions:\r\n\r\n  - `scipy.stats.dpareto_lognorm` represents the double Pareto lognormal\r\n    distribution.\r\n  - `scipy.stats.landau` represents the Landau distribution.\r\n  - `scipy.stats.normal_inverse_gamma` represents the normal-inverse-gamma\r\n    distribution.\r\n  - `scipy.stats.poisson_binom` represents the Poisson binomial distribution.\r\n\r\n- Batch calculation with `scipy.stats.alexandergovern` and\r\n  `scipy.stats.combine_pvalues` is faster.\r\n- `scipy.stats.chisquare` added an argument ``sum_check``. By default, the\r\n  function raises an error when the sum of expected and obseved frequencies\r\n  are not equal; setting ``sum_check=False`` disables this check to\r\n  facilitate hypothesis tests other than Pearson's chi-squared test.\r\n- The accuracy of several distribution methods has been improved, including:\r\n\r\n  - `scipy.stats.nct` method ``pdf``\r\n  - `scipy.stats.crystalball` method ``sf``\r\n  - `scipy.stats.geom` method ``rvs``\r\n  - `scipy.stats.cauchy` methods ``logpdf``, ``pdf``, ``ppf`` and ``isf``\r\n  - The ``logcdf`` and/or ``logsf`` methods of distributions that do not\r\n    override the generic implementation of these methods, including\r\n    `scipy.stats.beta`, `scipy.stats.betaprime`, `scipy.stats.cauchy`,\r\n    `scipy.stats.chi`, `scipy.stats.chi2`, `scipy.stats.exponweib`,\r\n    `scipy.stats.gamma`, `scipy.stats.gompertz`, `scipy.stats.halflogistic`,\r\n    `scipy.stats.hypsecant`, `scipy.stats.invgamma`, `scipy.stats.laplace`,\r\n    `scipy.stats.levy`, `scipy.stats.loggamma`, `scipy.stats.maxwell`,\r\n    `scipy.stats.nakagami`, and `scipy.stats.t`.\r\n\r\n- `scipy.stats.qmc.PoissonDisk` now accepts lower and upper bounds\r\n  parameters ``l_bounds`` and ``u_bounds``.\r\n- `scipy.stats.fisher_exact` now supports two-dimensional tables with shapes\r\n  other than ``(2, 2)``.\r\n\r\nPreliminary Support for Free-Threaded CPython 3.13\r\n========================================\r\nSciPy ``1.15`` has preliminary support for the free-threaded build of CPython\r\n``3.13.`` This allows SciPy functionality to execute in parallel with Python\r\nthreads\r\n(see the ``threading`` stdlib module). This support was enabled by fixing a\r\nsignificant number of thread-safety issues in both pure Python and\r\nC/C++/Cython/Fortran extension modules. Wheels are provided on PyPI for this\r\nrelease; NumPy ``>=2.1.3`` is required at runtime. Note that building for a\r\nfree-threaded interpreter requires a recent pre-release or nightly for Cython\r\n``3.1.0``.\r\n\r\nSupport for free-threaded Python does not mean that SciPy is fully thread-safe.\r\nPlease see :ref:`scipy_thread_safety` for more details.\r\n\r\nIf you are interested in free-threaded Python, for example because you have a\r\nmultiprocessing-based workflow that you are interested in running with Python\r\nthreads, we encourage testing and experimentation. If you run into problems\r\nthat you suspect are because of SciPy, please open an issue, checking first if\r\nthe bug also occurs in the \"regular\" non-free-threaded CPython ``3.13`` build.\r\nMany threading bugs can also occur in code that releases the GIL; disabling\r\nthe GIL only makes it easier to hit threading bugs.\r\n\r\n\r\nArray API Standard Support\r\n=====================\r\nExperimental support for array libraries other than NumPy has been added to\r\nexisting sub-packages in recent versions of SciPy. Please consider testing\r\nthese features by setting an environment variable ``SCIPY_ARRAY_API=1`` and\r\nproviding PyTorch, JAX, ndonnx, or CuPy arrays as array arguments. Features\r\nwith support added for SciPy ``1.15.0`` include:\r\n\r\n- All functions in `scipy.differentiate` (new sub-package)\r\n- All functions in `scipy.optimize.elementwise` (new namespace)\r\n- `scipy.optimize.rosen`, `scipy.optimize.rosen_der`, and\r\n  `scipy.optimize.rosen_hess`\r\n- `scipy.special.logsumexp`\r\n- `scipy.integrate.trapezoid`\r\n- `scipy.integrate.tanhsinh` (newly public function)\r\n- `scipy.integrate.cubature` (new function)\r\n- `scipy.integrate.nsum` (new function)\r\n- `scipy.special.chdtr`, `scipy.special.betainc`, and `scipy.special.betaincc`\r\n- `scipy.stats.boxcox_llf`\r\n- `scipy.stats.differential_entropy`\r\n- `scipy.stats.zmap`, `scipy.stats.zscore`, and `scipy.stats.gzscore`\r\n- `scipy.stats.tmean`, `scipy.stats.tvar`, `scipy.stats.tstd`,\r\n  `scipy.stats.tsem`, `scipy.stats.tmin`, and `scipy.stats.tmax`\r\n- `scipy.stats.gmean`, `scipy.stats.hmean` and `scipy.stats.pmean`\r\n- `scipy.stats.combine_pvalues`\r\n- `scipy.stats.ttest_ind`, `scipy.stats.ttest_rel`\r\n- `scipy.stats.directional_stats`\r\n- `scipy.ndimage` functions will now delegate to ``cupyx.scipy.ndimage``,\r\n  and for other backends will transit via NumPy arrays on the host.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nDeprecated features\r\n================\r\n- Functions `scipy.linalg.interpolative.rand` and\r\n  `scipy.linalg.interpolative.seed` have been deprecated and will be removed\r\n  in SciPy ``1.17.0``.\r\n- Complex inputs to `scipy.spatial.distance.cosine` and\r\n  `scipy.spatial.distance.correlation` have been deprecated and will raise\r\n  an error in SciPy ``1.17.0``.\r\n- `scipy.spatial.distance.kulczynski1` and\r\n  `scipy.spatial.distance.sokalmichener` were deprecated and will be removed\r\n  in SciPy ``1.17.0``.\r\n- `scipy.stats.find_repeats` is deprecated as of SciPy ``1.15.0`` and will be\r\n  removed in SciPy ``1.17.0``. Please use\r\n  ``numpy.unique``/``numpy.unique_counts`` instead.\r\n- `scipy.linalg.kron` is deprecated in favour of ``numpy.kron``.\r\n- Using object arrays and longdouble arrays in `scipy.signal`\r\n  convolution/correlation functions (`scipy.signal.correlate`,\r\n  `scipy.signal.convolve` and `scipy.signal.choose_conv_method`) and\r\n  filtering functions (`scipy.signal.lfilter`, `scipy.signal.sosfilt`) has\r\n  been deprecated as of SciPy ``1.15.0`` and will be removed in SciPy\r\n  ``1.17.0``.\r\n- `scipy.stats.linregress` has deprecated one-argument use; the two\r\n  variables must be specified as separate arguments.\r\n- ``scipy.stats.trapz`` is deprecated in favor of `scipy.stats.trapezoid`.\r\n- `scipy.special.lpn` is deprecated in favor of `scipy.special.legendre_p_all`.\r\n- `scipy.special.lpmn` and `scipy.special.clpmn` are deprecated in favor of\r\n  `scipy.special.assoc_legendre_p_all`.\r\n- The raveling of multi-dimensional input by `scipy.linalg.toeplitz` has\r\n  been deprecated. It will support batching in SciPy ``1.17.0``.\r\n- The ``random_state`` and ``permutations`` arguments of\r\n  `scipy.stats.ttest_ind` are deprecated. Use ``method`` to perform a\r\n  permutation test, instead.\r\n\r\n\r\nExpired Deprecations\r\n================\r\n- The wavelet functions in `scipy.signal` have been removed. This includes\r\n  ``daub``, ``qmf``, ``cascade``, ``morlet``, ``morlet2``, ``ricker``,\r\n  and ``cwt``. Users should use ``pywavelets`` instead.\r\n- ``scipy.signal.cmplx_sort`` has been removed.\r\n- ``scipy.integrate.quadrature`` and ``scipy.integrate.romberg`` have been\r\n  removed in favour of `scipy.integrate.quad`.\r\n- ``scipy.stats.rvs_ratio_uniforms`` has been removed in favor of\r\n  `scipy.stats.sampling.RatioUniforms`.\r\n- `scipy.special.factorial` now raises an error for non-integer scalars when\r\n  ``exact=True``.\r\n- `scipy.integrate.cumulative_trapezoid` now raises an error for values of\r\n  ``initial`` other than ``0`` and ``None``.\r\n- Complex dtypes now raise an error in `scipy.interpolate.Akima1DInterpolator`\r\n  and `scipy.interpolate.PchipInterpolator`\r\n- ``special.btdtr`` and ``special.btdtri`` have been removed.\r\n- The default of the ``exact=`` kwarg in ``special.factorialk`` has changed\r\n  from ``True`` to ``False``.\r\n- All functions in the ``scipy.misc`` submodule have been removed.\r\n\r\n\r\nBackwards incompatible changes\r\n==========================\r\n- ``interpolate.BSpline.integrate`` output is now always a numpy array.\r\n  Previously, for 1D splines the output was a python float or a 0D array\r\n  depending on the value of the ``extrapolate`` argument.\r\n- `scipy.stats.wilcoxon` now respects the ``method`` argument provided by the\r\n  user. Previously, even if ``method='exact'`` was specified, the function\r\n  would resort to ``method='approx'`` in some cases.\r\n\r\n\r\nOther changes\r\n============\r\n- A separate accompanying type stubs package, ``scipy-stubs``, will be made\r\n  available with the ``1.15.0`` release. `Installation instructions are\r\n  available\r\n  <https://github.com/jorenham/scipy-stubs?tab=readme-ov-file#installation>`_.\r\n- `scipy.stats.bootstrap` now emits a ``FutureWarning`` if the shapes of the\r\n  input arrays do not agree. Broadcast the arrays to the same batch shape\r\n  (i.e. for all dimensions except those specified by the ``axis`` argument)\r\n  to avoid the warning. Broadcasting will be performed automatically in the\r\n  future.\r\n- SciPy endorsed `SPEC-7 <https://scientific-python.org/specs/spec-0007/>`_,\r\n  which proposes a ``rng`` argument to control pseudorandom number generation\r\n  (PRNG) in a standard way, replacing legacy arguments like ``seed`` and\r\n  ``random_sate``. In many cases, use of ``rng`` will change the behavior of\r\n  the function unless the argument is already an instance of\r\n  ``numpy.random.Generator``.\r\n\r\n  - Effective in SciPy ``1.15.0``:\r\n\r\n    - The ``rng`` argument has been added to the following functions:\r\n      `scipy.cluster.vq.kmeans`, `scipy.cluster.vq.kmeans2`,\r\n      `scipy.interpolate.BarycentricInterpolator`,\r\n      `scipy.interpolate.barycentric_interpolate`,\r\n      `scipy.linalg.clarkson_woodruff_transform`,\r\n      `scipy.optimize.basinhopping`,\r\n      `scipy.optimize.differential_evolution`, `scipy.optimize.dual_annealing`,\r\n      `scipy.optimize.check_grad`, `scipy.optimize.quadratic_assignment`,\r\n      `scipy.sparse.random`, `scipy.sparse.random_array`, `scipy.sparse.rand`,\r\n      `scipy.sparse.linalg.svds`, `scipy.spatial.transform.Rotation.random`,\r\n      `scipy.spatial.distance.directed_hausdorff`,\r\n      `scipy.stats.goodness_of_fit`, `scipy.stats.BootstrapMethod`,\r\n      `scipy.stats.PermutationMethod`, `scipy.stats.bootstrap`,\r\n      `scipy.stats.permutation_test`, `scipy.stats.dunnett`, all\r\n      `scipy.stats.qmc` classes that consume random numbers, and\r\n      `scipy.stats.sobol_indices`.\r\n    - When passed by keyword, the ``rng`` argument will follow the SPEC 7\r\n      standard behavior: the argument will be normalized with\r\n      ``np.random.default_rng`` before being used.\r\n    - When passed by position or legacy keyword, the behavior of the argument\r\n      will remain unchanged (for now).\r\n\r\n  - It is planned that in ``1.17.0`` the legacy argument will start emitting\r\n    warnings, and that in ``1.19.0`` the default behavior will change.\r\n  - In all cases, users can avoid future disruption by proactively passing\r\n    an instance of ``np.random.Generator`` by keyword ``rng``. For details,\r\n    see `SPEC-7 <https://scientific-python.org/specs/spec-0007/>`_.\r\n\r\n- The SciPy build no longer adds ``-std=legacy`` for Fortran code,\r\n  except when using Gfortran. This avoids problems with the new Flang and\r\n  AMD Fortran compilers. It may make new build warnings appear for other\r\n  compilers - if so, please file an issue.\r\n\r\n\r\n- ``scipy.signal.sosfreqz`` has been renamed to `scipy.signal.freqz_sos`.\r\n  New code should use the new name. The old name is maintained as an alias for\r\n  backwards compatibility.\r\n- Testing thread-safety improvements related to Python ``3.13t`` have been\r\n  made in: `scipy.special`, `scipy.spatial`, `scipy.sparse`,\r\n  `scipy.interpolate`.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nAuthors (commits)\r\n==============\r\n\r\n* endolith (4)\r\n* h-vetinari (61)\r\n* a-drenaline (1) +\r\n* Afleloup (1) +\r\n* Ahmad Alkadri (1) +\r\n* Luiz Eduardo Amaral (3) +\r\n* Virgile Andreani (3)\r\n* Isaac Alonso Asensio (2) +\r\n* Matteo Bachetti (1) +\r\n* Arash Badie-Modiri (1) +\r\n* Arnaud Baguet (1) +\r\n* Soutrik Bandyopadhyay (1) +\r\n* Ankit Barik (1) +\r\n* Christoph Baumgarten (1)\r\n* Nickolai Belakovski (3)\r\n* Krishan Bhasin (1) +\r\n* Jake Bowhay (85)\r\n* Michael Bratsch (2) +\r\n* Matthew Brett (1)\r\n* Keith Briggs (1) +\r\n* Olly Britton (145) +\r\n* Dietrich Brunn (11)\r\n* Clemens Brunner (1)\r\n* Evgeni Burovski (182)\r\n* Matthias Bussonnier (7)\r\n* CJ Carey (32)\r\n* Cesar Carrasco (4) +\r\n* Hood Chatham (1)\r\n* Aadya Chinubhai (1)\r\n* Alessandro Chitarrini (1) +\r\n* Thibault de Coincy (1) +\r\n* Lucas Colley (217)\r\n* Martin Diehl (1) +\r\n* Djip007 (1) +\r\n* Kevin Doshi (2) +\r\n* Michael Dunphy (2)\r\n* Andy Everall (1) +\r\n* Thomas J. #### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This fixes a regression in the titles of plots produced by the `plot` method of distributions from the new infrastructure that was pointed out here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22025#discussion_r1884759224. It was introduced in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22037, because we used the more informative `__repr__` overrides for `__str__`, with the unintended consequence that obtrusive datatype info appears in the plot titles like this:

![normal_plot](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e866c2ab-6b43-4ac3-ab43-4d9eea4f34b2)

To fix this, I've defined `__str__` overrides which are just direct copy pastes of the `__repr__` overrides, but with `repr` replaced by `str` throughout. I also needed to update `MonotonicTransformedDistribution` to take a `str_pattern` in addition to `repr_pattern`, because there were some cases where this is needed.

With these changes, the above plot looks like this:

![normal_plot2](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/12c956f1-73d7-4f29-9645-8108607ab293)

I also updated some tests for `__str__` which were assuming `str` was the same as `repr`.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->

We could do something fancier to reduce code duplication, but since it's really pushing it whether this can be backported, I figured it would be better to put in the easiest thing to confirm is correct.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/22081/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/22081/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2543348749","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22081#issuecomment-2543348749","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/22081","id":2543348749,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6XmGwN","user":{"login":"tylerjereddy","id":7903078,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5MDMwNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7903078?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy","html_url":"https://github.com/tylerjereddy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-14T21:23:57Z","updated_at":"2024-12-14T21:23:57Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Ok, I added a tentative backport label. {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/comments/1889424035","pull_request_review_id":2510484629,"id":1889424035,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIoc5wnlKj","diff_hunk":"@@ -234,25 +239,27 @@ and support several Array API compatible array libraries in addition to NumPy
   GPUs.
 - `scipy.special.ndtr` is now more efficient.
 
-`scipy.stats` improvements
-==========================
+``scipy.stats`` improvements
+============================
 - A new probability distribution infrastructure has been added for the
-  implementation of univariate, continuous distributions with speed,
-  accuracy, and memory advantages:
+  implementation of univariate, continuous distributions. It has several
+  speed, accuracy, memory, and interface advantages compared to the
+  previous infrastructure. See :ref:`rv_infrastructure` for a tutorial.
 
-  - `scipy.stats.Normal` represents the normal distribution with the new
-    interface. In typical cases, its methods are faster and more accurate than
-    those of `scipy.stats.norm`.
   - Use `scipy.stats.make_distribution` to treat an existing continuous
     distribution (e.g. `scipy.stats.norm`) with the new infrastructure.
     This can improve the speed and accuracy of existing distributions,
-    especially for methods not overridden with custom formulas in the
-    implementation.
-
-- `scipy.stats.Mixture` has been added to represent mixture distributions.
-- Instances of `scipy.stats.Normal` and the classes returned by
-  `scipy.stats.make_distribution` are supported by several new mathematical
-  transformations.
+    especially those with methods not overridden with distribution-specific
+    formulas.
+  - `scipy.stats.Normal` and `scipy.stats.Uniform` are pre-defined classes
+    to represents the normal and uniform distributions, respectively.","path":"doc/source/release/1.15.0-notes.rst","commit_id":"37bcd6e6b3b628497aacfad72d62d33c07d8a5f6","original_commit_id":"37bcd6e6b3b628497aacfad72d62d33c07d8a5f6","user":{"login":"tylerjereddy","id":7903078,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5MDMwNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7903078?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy","html_url":"https://github.com/tylerjereddy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"`represents` -> `represent` I think?

What does this implement/fix?
A few other updates to the release notes:
- Organizes the highlights related to vectorized/ Array API compatible new features into a top-level bullet
- Reorders `integrate` improvements
- Improves note about new distribution infrastructure

Also, Iupdated table of contents entries to link to the appropriate section of the release notes rather than the API documentation of the subpackage. ~~It looks like the reason this doesn't happen automatically is that the section headings already include a link. As an experiment, I'm seeing whether this works if the sub-package names are just monospaced font rather than links. If this doesn't work, any ideas?~~ It worked! 

Do others agree this would be more expected behavior and more useful?

What does this implement/fix?
A few other updates to the release notes:
- Organizes the highlights related to vectorized/ Array API compatible new features into a top-level bullet
- Reorders `integrate` improvements
- Improves note about new distribution infrastructure

Also, Iupdated table of contents entries to link to the appropriate section of the release notes rather than the API documentation of the subpackage. ~~It looks like the reason this doesn't happen automatically is that the section headings already include a link. As an experiment, I'm seeing whether this works if the sub-package names are just monospaced font rather than links. If this doesn't work, any ideas?~~ It worked! 

Do others agree this would be more expected behavior and more useful?

A few other updates to the release notes:
- Organizes the highlights related to vectorized/ Array API compatible new features into a top-level bullet
- Reorders `integrate` improvements
- Improves note about new distribution infrastructure

Also, Iupdated table of contents entries to link to the appropriate section of the release notes rather than the API documentation of the subpackage. ~~It looks like the reason this doesn't happen automatically is that the section headings already include a link. As an experiment, I'm seeing whether this works if the sub-package names are just monospaced font rather than links. If this doesn't work, any ideas?~~ It worked! 

Do others agree this would be more expected behavior and more useful?

ody":"#### What does this implement/fix?
A few other updates to the release notes:
- Organizes the highlights related to vectorized/ Array API compatible new features into a top-level bullet
- Reorders `integrate` improvements
- Improves note about new distribution infrastructure

Also, Iupdated table of contents entries to link to the appropriate section of the release notes rather than the API documentation of the subpackage. ~~It looks like the reason this doesn't happen automatically is that the section headings already include a link. As an experiment, I'm seeing whether this works if the sub-package names are just monospaced font rather than links. If this doesn't work, any ideas?~~ It worked! 

Do others agree this would be more expected behavior and more useful?

:"* Don't merge until the `1.15.0` release notes updates have been merged and the `maintenance/1.15.x` branch has been pushed up (I'll probably merge when the time is right).

* The changed dependency versions in the `1.16.0` release notes are just drafts/subject to change. Note that I've not taken on the task of actually updating those dependencies in places where they may get checked programmatically (i.e., `pyproject.toml`), since that may be a larger project that can happen after branching.

* It looks like our version handling has actually simplified since the previous release--see the matching PR from previous release at gh-20827.

[docs only]

Reference issue
gh-22104

#### What does this implement/fix?
Updates the release notes for 1.16 to ensure that the TOC links to section headings below rather than the API documentation.

Also updates minimum NumPy version to that which will be recommended by [SPEC 0](https://scientific-python.org/specs/spec-0000/), assuming we release in late June like last year. I imagine this will be consider too agressive of a jump, so perhaps we split the difference: 1.25.2 in SciPy 1.16.0, and NumPy 2.0.1 in SciPy 1.17.0?

gh-22104

#### What does this implement/fix?
Updates the release notes for 1.16 to ensure that the TOC links to section headings below rather than the API documentation.

Also updates minimum NumPy version to that which will be recommended by [SPEC 0](https://scientific-python.org/specs/spec-0000/), assuming we release in late June like last year. I imagine this will be consider too agressive of a jump, so perhaps we split the difference: 1.25.2 in SciPy 1.16.0, and NumPy 2.0.1 in SciPy 1.17.0?

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/pulls/comments/1890969841","pull_request_review_id":2513077361,"id":1890969841,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIq85wtejx","diff_hunk":"@@ -3,101 +3,206 @@ title: Installation sidebar: false --- -Installations methods include: +The recommended method of installing SciPy depends on your preferred workflow. +The common workflows can roughly be broken down into the following +categories: -- [Distributions](#distributions) -- [pip](#pip-install) -- [conda](#pip-install) -- [Package Manager](#package_manager) -- [Source](#source) +- [Project-based (e.g. `uv`, `pixi`)](#project-based) (recommended for new users) +- [Environment-based (e.g. `pip`, `conda`)](#environment-based) (the traditional workflow) +- [System package managers](#system-package-managers) (not recommended) +- [Building from source](#building-from-source) (for debugging and development) -Methods differ in ease of use, coverage, maintenance of old versions, -system-wide versus local environment use, and control. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Project-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In project-based workflows, a project is a directory containing a manifest +file describing the project, a lock-file describing the exact dependencies +of the project, and the project's (potentially multiple) environments. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +### Installing with `uv` -Other options include: +{{< admonition tip >}} +If it is your first time installing a Python library, start here! +{{< /admonition >}} -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +Here is a step-by-step guide to setting up a project to try SciPy with [`uv`], +a Python package manager. - +[`uv`]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/ -# Installing with Pip +1. Install `uv`, following [the instructions in the `uv` documentation][install-uv]. -You can install SciPy from PyPI with `pip`: +[install-uv]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/installation/ - python -m pip install scipy +2. Create a new project in a new subdirectory, by executing the following in a terminal: - + uv init try-scipy + cd try-scipy -# Installing via Conda + {{< admonition hint >}} +The second command changes directory into the directory of your project. + {{< /admonition >}} -You can install SciPy from the `defaults` or `conda-forge` channels with `conda`: +3. Install Python: - conda install scipy + uv python install - + {{< admonition note >}} +If you have already installed Python, this step is optional. + {{< /admonition >}} -# Install system-wide via a package manager +4. Add SciPy to your project: + + uv add scipy + +5. Try out SciPy! + + uv run python + + This will launch a Python interpreter session, from which you can `import scipy`. + +See next steps in [the SciPy user guide][scipy-user-guide]. + +[scipy-user-guide]: https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/tutorial/ + +### Installing with `pixi` + +If you work with non-Python packages, you may prefer to install SciPy as +a [Conda] package, so that you can use the same workflow for packages which +are not available on [PyPI](https://pypi.org/), the Python Package Index. +Conda can manage packages in any language, so you can use it to install +Python itself, compilers, and other languages. + +[Conda]: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/index.html + +The steps to install SciPy from [conda-forge] using the package management +tool [`pixi`] are very similar to the steps for `uv`: + +[conda-forge]: https://conda-forge.org/ +[`pixi`]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ + +1. Install `pixi`, following [the instructions in the `pixi` documentation][install-pixi]. + +[install-pixi]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ + +2. Create a new project in a new subdirectory: + + pixi init try-scipy + cd try-scipy + +3. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Project-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In project-based workflows, a project is a directory containing a manifest +file describing the project, a lock-file describing the exact dependencies +of the project, and the project's (potentially multiple) environments. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +### Installing with `uv` -Other options include: +{{< admonition tip >}} +If it is your first time installing a Python library, start here! +{{< /admonition >}} -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +Here is a step-by-step guide to setting up a project to try SciPy with [`uv`], +a Python package manager. - +[`uv`]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/ -# Installing with Pip +1. Install `uv`, following [the instructions in the `uv` documentation][install-uv]. -You can install SciPy from PyPI with `pip`: +[install-uv]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/installation/ - python -m pip install scipy +2. Create a new project in a new subdirectory, by executing the following in a terminal: - + uv init try-scipy + cd try-scipy -# Installing via Conda + {{< admonition hint >}} +The second command changes directory into the directory of your project. + {{< /admonition >}} -You can install SciPy from the `defaults` or `conda-forge` channels with `conda`: +3. Install Python: - conda install scipy + uv python install - + {{< admonition note >}} +If you have already installed Python, this step is optional. + {{< /admonition >}} -# Install system-wide via a package manager +4. Add SciPy to your project: + + uv add scipy + +5. Try out SciPy! + + uv run python + + This will launch a Python interpreter session, from which you can `import scipy`. + +See next steps in [the SciPy user guide][scipy-user-guide]. + +[scipy-user-guide]: https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/tutorial/ + +### Installing with `pixi` + +If you work with non-Python packages, you may prefer to install SciPy as +a [Conda] package, so that you can use the same workflow for packages which +are not available on [PyPI](https://pypi.org/), the Python Package Index. +Conda can manage packages in any language, so you can use it to install +Python itself, compilers, and other languages. + +[Conda]: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/index.html + +The steps to install SciPy from [conda-forge] using the package management +tool [`pixi`] are very similar to the steps for `uv`: + +[conda-forge]: https://conda-forge.org/ +[`pixi`]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ + +1. Install `pixi`, following [the instructions in the `pixi` documentation][install-pixi]. + +[install-pixi]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ + +2. {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/pulls/comments/1890976962","pull_request_review_id":2513087540,"id":1890976962,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIq85wtgTC","diff_hunk":"@@ -3,101 +3,206 @@ title: Installation sidebar: false --- -Installations methods include: +The recommended method of installing SciPy depends on your preferred workflow. +The common workflows can roughly be broken down into the following +categories: -- [Distributions](#distributions) -- [pip](#pip-install) -- [conda](#pip-install) -- [Package Manager](#package_manager) -- [Source](#source) +- [Project-based (e.g. `uv`, `pixi`)](#project-based) (recommended for new users) +- [Environment-based (e.g. `pip`, `conda`)](#environment-based) (the traditional workflow) +- [System package managers](#system-package-managers) (not recommended) +- [Building from source](#building-from-source) (for debugging and development) -Methods differ in ease of use, coverage, maintenance of old versions, -system-wide versus local environment use, and control. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Project-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In project-based workflows, a project is a directory containing a manifest +file describing the project, a lock-file describing the exact dependencies +of the project, and the project's (potentially multiple) environments. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +### Installing with `uv` -Other options include: +{{< admonition tip >}} +If it is your first time installing a Python library, start here! +{{< /admonition >}} -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +Here is a step-by-step guide to setting up a project to try SciPy with [`uv`], +a Python package manager. - +[`uv`]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/ -# Installing with Pip +1. Install `uv`, following [the instructions in the `uv` documentation][install-uv]. -You can install SciPy from PyPI with `pip`: +[install-uv]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/installation/ - python -m pip install scipy +2. Create a new project in a new subdirectory, by executing the following in a terminal: - + uv init try-scipy + cd try-scipy -# Installing via Conda + {{< admonition hint >}} +The second command changes directory into the directory of your project. + {{< /admonition >}} -You can install SciPy from the `defaults` or `conda-forge` channels with `conda`: +3. Install Python: - conda install scipy + uv python install - + {{< admonition note >}} +If you have already installed Python, this step is optional. + {{< /admonition >}} -# Install system-wide via a package manager +4. Add SciPy to your project: + + uv add scipy + +5. Try out SciPy! + + uv run python + + This will launch a Python interpreter session, from which you can `import scipy`. + +See next steps in [the SciPy user guide][scipy-user-guide]. + +[scipy-user-guide]: https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/tutorial/ + +### Installing with `pixi` + +If you work with non-Python packages, you may prefer to install SciPy as +a [Conda] package, so that you can use the same workflow for packages which +are not available on [PyPI](https://pypi.org/), the Python Package Index. +Conda can manage packages in any language, so you can use it to install +Python itself, compilers, and other languages. + +[Conda]: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/index.html + +The steps to install SciPy from [conda-forge] using the package management +tool [`pixi`] are very similar to the steps for `uv`: + +[conda-forge]: https://conda-forge.org/ +[`pixi`]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ + +1. Install `pixi`, following [the instructions in the `pixi` documentation][install-pixi]. + +[install-pixi]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ + +2. Create a new project in a new subdirectory: + + pixi init try-scipy + cd try-scipy + +3. Add SciPy to your project: + + uv add Reference issue
Closes gh-22146

#### What does this implement/fix?
Code for an `llf` method has been hiding in the `ContinuousDistribution` class, but the documentation didn't render and it's not tested because it wasn't supposed to be public at this time. This PR removes the method for now.

#### Additional information

Describe your issue.

`BSpline` fails to run after unpickling with `mmap_mode="r"`.
This is a regression in the release candidate `scipy==1.15.0rc1`, and does not happen in `scipy==1.14.0`, and it was originally spotted in scikit-lego where we run test suite against release candidates as well.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import tempfile
import joblib
from pathlib import Path
from scipy.interpolate import BSpline

# from the example in the docs
k = 2
t = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
c = [-1, 2, 0, -1]
estimator = BSpline(t=t, c=c, k=k)

temp_folder = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="tmp")
filename = Path(temp_folder) / "bspline.pkl"
joblib.dump(estimator, filename)

unpickled_estimator = joblib.load(filename, mmap_mode="r")
unpickled_estimator([1,2,3])  # <--- this call will fail
```

<details>
<summary> Originally code that spotted the issue </summary>

```py
from sklearn.preprocessing import SplineTransformer
from sklearn.utils.estimator_checks import check_estimators_pickle

check_estimators_pickle(
    name="hello",
    estimator_orig=SplineTransformer(),
    readonly_memmap=True,
)
```
</details>


### Error message

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/fbruzzesi/open-source/scikit-lego/t.py", line 21, in <module>
    unpickled_estimator([1,2,3])
  File "/home/fbruzzesi/open-source/scikit-lego/.venv-pre/lib/python3.10/site-packages/scipy/interpolate/_bsplines.py", line 523, in __call__
    _dierckx.evaluate_spline(self.t, cc.reshape(cc.shape[0], -1),
ValueError: Expected a 1-dim C contiguous array  of dtype = 12( got 12 )
```

On this feature branch, I didn't see any signs of hanging/slow performance, but I still observed some test failures (157 in total).

Most, perhaps all, of the failures are related to failure to issue a warning in a `stats` test and IIRC warnings handling has thread safety issues?

Regardless, here is a sample of one the fails I see locally:


On this feature branch, I didn't see any signs of hanging/slow performance, but I still observed some test failures (157 in total).

Most, perhaps all, of the failures are related to failure to issue a warning in a `stats` test and IIRC warnings handling has thread safety issues?

Regardless, here is a sample of one the fails I see locally:

```
_______________________________________________________________________ TestVariation.test_combined_edge_cases[omit-numpy] ________________________________________________________________________
[gw5] darwin -- Python 3.13.0 /Users/treddy/python_venvs/py_313_free_threaded/bin/python
scipy/stats/tests/test_variation.py:173: in test_combined_edge_cases
    with pytest.warns(SmallSampleWarning, match=too_small_nd_omit):
E   Failed: DID NOT WARN. No warnings of type (<class 'scipy.stats._axis_nan_policy.SmallSampleWarning'>,) were emitted.
E    Emitted warnings: [].
        nan_policy = 'omit'
        self       = <scipy.stats.tests.test_variation.TestVariation object at 0x2eee809b170>
        x          = array([[ 0., 10., nan,  1.],
       [ 0., -5., nan,  2.],
       [ 0., -5., nan,  3.]])
        xp         = <module 'numpy' from '/Users/treddy/python_venvs/py_313_free_threaded/lib/python3.13t/site-packages/numpy/__init__.py'>
        y          = array([       nan,        inf,        nan, 0.40824829])
```

Reference issue
Fix https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/22143

#### What does this implement/fix?
This uses `PyArray_Check` rather than `PyArray_CheckExact` in order to accept ndarray subclasses at least that's my understanding of the [C API doc](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/c-api/array.html#general-check-of-python-type).

The reason for this is than in the scikit-learn tests `obj` can be a `np.memmap` which is a subclass of `ndarray`.

And it is described in the release notes in a bullet on dtype checking.

hopefully completes the work from #20754 and the issue #20207 to implement dtype checking in sparse constructors. We discovered some cases where the constructor logic was avoiding the checks. This covers the cases:

- dense input to COO with invalid data dtype (this also affects CSR, CSC, DIA, BSR which call COO as part of handling dense input)
- sparse input to BSR with invalid dtype keyword  

I've added tests for these cases on all formats and added dtype checking in COO and BSR.

The COO code removes the check at the end of data construction because that code wasn't checking the faulty `dtype is None` case and it duplicated checking done in the if/else logic structure. I moved the check inside the if/else structure where needed. The dense branch didn't have any coverage when `dtype is None`. It just used the incoming dense dtype even if not valid for sparse (i.e. float16, python object, etc). The sparse input branch needed checks. All others were already covered. Now all branches of the if/else structure check for a valid dtype.

The BSR change is pretty straightforward.

This should be backported to 1.15 because it fills in missed spots in this new feature of 1.15. And it is described in the release notes in a bullet on dtype #### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
This is a promised follow-up to #22156 

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds a deprecation warning for `sph_harm`. It is done by calling [PyErr_WarnEx](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_WarnEx) from within the wrapper for `sph_harm` inside of `scipy/special/xsf_special.h`. This header is intended for SciPy specific modifications of `xsf` code, and as it turns, `sph_harm` already had a wrapper there in order to have a warning printed when floating point values are passed for arguments which should be integers.

I've also suppressed the warnings in the tests and added a test to check that the deprecation warning appears.

In addition, I've also added comments explaining why we specifically left in tests for the deprecated functions instead of updating them to use the new function, as promised here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22156#discussion_r1895081141.

cc @h-vetinari, @jorenham

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
This is a promised follow-up to #22156 

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds a deprecation warning for `sph_harm`. It is done by calling [PyErr_WarnEx](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_WarnEx) from within the wrapper for `sph_harm` inside of `scipy/special/xsf_special.h`. This header is intended for SciPy specific modifications of `xsf` code, and as it turns, `sph_harm` already had a wrapper there in order to have a warning printed when floating point values are passed for arguments which should be integers.

I've also suppressed the warnings in the tests and added a test to check that the deprecation warning appears.

In addition, I've also added comments explaining why we specifically left in tests for the deprecated functions instead of updating them to use the new function, as promised here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22156#discussion_r1895081141.

cc @h-vetinari, @jorenham

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
This is a promised follow-up to #22156 

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds a deprecation warning for `sph_harm`. It is done by calling [PyErr_WarnEx](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_WarnEx) from within the wrapper for `sph_harm` inside of `scipy/special/xsf_special.h`. This header is intended for SciPy specific modifications of `xsf` code, and as it turns, `sph_harm` already had a wrapper there in order to have a warning printed when floating point values are passed for arguments which should be integers.

I've also suppressed the warnings in the tests and added a test to check that the deprecation warning appears.

In addition, I've also added comments explaining why we specifically left in tests for the deprecated functions instead of updating them to use the new function, as promised here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22156#discussion_r1895081141.

cc @h-vetinari, @jorenham

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
This is a promised follow-up to #22156 

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds a deprecation warning for `sph_harm`. It is done by calling [PyErr_WarnEx](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_WarnEx) from within the wrapper for `sph_harm` inside of `scipy/special/xsf_special.h`. This header is intended for SciPy specific modifications of `xsf` code, and as it turns, `sph_harm` already had a wrapper there in order to have a warning printed when floating point values are passed for arguments which should be integers.

I've also suppressed the warnings in the tests and added a test to check that the deprecation warning appears.

In addition, I've also added comments explaining why we specifically left in tests for the deprecated functions instead of updating them to use the new function, as promised here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22156#discussion_r1895081141.

cc @h-vetinari, @jorenham

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
This is a promised follow-up to #22156 

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds a deprecation warning for `sph_harm`. It is done by calling [PyErr_WarnEx](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_WarnEx) from within the wrapper for `sph_harm` inside of `scipy/special/xsf_special.h`. This header is intended for SciPy specific modifications of `xsf` code, and as it turns, `sph_harm` already had a wrapper there in order to have a warning printed when floating point values are passed for arguments which should be integers.

I've also suppressed the warnings in the tests and added a test to check that the deprecation warning appears.

In addition, I've also added comments explaining why we specifically left in tests for the deprecated functions instead of updating them to use the new function, as promised here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22156#discussion_r1895081141.

cc @h-vetinari, @jorenham

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
This is a promised follow-up to #22156 

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds a deprecation warning for `sph_harm`. It is done by calling [PyErr_WarnEx](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_WarnEx) from within the wrapper for `sph_harm` inside of `scipy/special/xsf_special.h`. This header is intended for SciPy specific modifications of `xsf` code, and as it turns, `sph_harm` already had a wrapper there in order to have a warning printed when floating point values are passed for arguments which should be integers.

I've also suppressed the warnings in the tests and added a test to check that the deprecation warning appears.

In addition, I've also added comments explaining why we specifically left in tests for the deprecated functions instead of updating them to use the new function, as promised here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22156#discussion_r1895081141.

cc @h-vetinari, @jorenham

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
This is a promised follow-up to #22156 

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds a deprecation warning for `sph_harm`. It is done by calling [PyErr_WarnEx](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_WarnEx) from within the wrapper for `sph_harm` inside of `scipy/special/xsf_special.h`. This header is intended for SciPy specific modifications of `xsf` code, and as it turns, `sph_harm` already had a wrapper there in order to have a warning printed when floating point values are passed for arguments which should be integers.

I've also suppressed the warnings in the tests and added a test to check that the deprecation warning appears.

In addition, I've also added comments explaining why we specifically left in tests for the deprecated functions instead of updating them to use the new function, as promised here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22156#discussion_r1895081141.

cc @h-vetinari, @jorenham

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
This is a promised follow-up to #22156 

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds a deprecation warning for `sph_harm`. It is done by calling [PyErr_WarnEx](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_WarnEx) from within the wrapper for `sph_harm` inside of `scipy/special/xsf_special.h`. This header is intended for SciPy specific modifications of `xsf` code, and as it turns, `sph_harm` already had a wrapper there in order to have a warning printed when floating point values are passed for arguments which should be integers.

I've also suppressed the warnings in the tests and added a test to check that the deprecation warning appears.

In addition, I've also added comments explaining why we specifically left in tests for the deprecated functions instead of updating them to use the new function, as promised here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22156#discussion_r1895081141.

cc @h-vetinari, @jorenham

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
This is a promised follow-up to #22156 

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds a deprecation warning for `sph_harm`. It is done by calling [PyErr_WarnEx](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_WarnEx) from within the wrapper for `sph_harm` inside of `scipy/special/xsf_special.h`. This header is intended for SciPy specific modifications of `xsf` code, and as it turns, `sph_harm` already had a wrapper there in order to have a warning printed when floating point values are passed for arguments which should be integers.

I've also suppressed the warnings in the tests and added a test to check that the deprecation warning appears.

In addition, I've also added comments explaining why we specifically left in tests for the deprecated functions instead of updating them to use the new function, as promised here: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22156#discussion_r1895081141.

cc @h-vetinari, @jorenham 


#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->

{"repository_id":1460385,"push_id":21845183871,"size":3,"distinct_size":3,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"f464cc12330df75043bd85a73655f19f6ea18ee3","before":"319ee60ca4a74766ee6605b191e2b0a34803d282","commits":[{"sha":"e4ff46fecbbd337796c5bf2220f41aa323625a92","author":{"email":"d1fe3f666890b04a41b0d38de55781d3a857620e@gmail.com","name":"Ralf Gommers"},"message":"CI: use Pythran master to build free-threaded wheels [wheel build]

Marking extension modules as thread-safe will only be available
in Pythran 0.17.1, and that is not yet released. This is the last
change needed to ensure the GIL isn't re-enabled on import.

Also add a CI check that no new extension modules without declared
free-threading support sneak back in.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/commits/e4ff46fecbbd337796c5bf2220f41aa323625a92"},{"sha":"c9308e2e9842270a03bfe68881bca476e4fe6dee","author":{"email":"d1fe3f666890b04a41b0d38de55781d3a857620e@gmail.com","name":"Ralf Gommers"},"message":"BUG: interpolate: fix missing free-threading support in `_dierckxmodule.cc`

for `_dierckx`.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/commits/c9308e2e9842270a03bfe68881bca476e4fe6dee"},{"sha":"f464cc12330df75043bd85a73655f19f6ea18ee3","author":{"email":"05cdbc50859d0142332b1c0e4edb7acdfab51a3a@gmail.com","name":"Tyler Reddy"},"message":"Merge pull request #22177 from rgommers/nogil-pythran-fix

{"repository_id":1460385,"push_id":21845272801,"size":2,"distinct_size":2,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"f40d2cd27d46efc6090e6612f19fc720a5ea48e2","before":"f464cc12330df75043bd85a73655f19f6ea18ee3","commits":[{"sha":"978a047e6dafb17441b5a3c59f8c9e90d031ee04","author":{"email":"d1fe3f666890b04a41b0d38de55781d3a857620e@gmail.com","name":"Ralf Gommers"},"message":"MAINT: stats: fix thread-safety issues under free-threaded CPython

future, so no need to do more for it that a lock now.
